title: Lesson 375
transcript: Hi, welcome back for another video. This one deals with how a competent security professional needs to be able to, first of all, understand and secondly, apply security concepts. Now, I think all of us will feel familiar with the contents of this one. You know, traditionally, we've referred to security as being three things, confidentiality, integrity and availability. In fact, for the longest time, people have referred to those as the CIA triad, although nobody understands what that means and we shouldn't refer it. using those three letters. But the way the industry has evolved and what security has to focus on these days, first of all, includes those three focuses of security, but much more as well. In fact, over the last number of years, there are some really important focuses of organizations to not only protect all valuable assets of the organization from obviously the perspective of confidentiality, integrity, and availability, but also two others. Those two others are authenticity and non-repudiation. So we can say, and the CISSP and certainly the industry, because everything that the CISSP does is really focused on what the industry does. Today, security has to focus on five important security services. And that's what this section deals with. And like I said, I think we're all familiar with these, but let's explain them from the perspective of, what the common body of knowledge of security really requires and therefore the exam outline and exam requires as well. So typically these have been referred to as the three of security, for example, but there are now five that you can see here, right? So when we focus on security services, As competent security professionals, we need to understand that we're focusing on confidentiality, integrity, availability, but also authenticity and non-repudiation. And one important thing to highlight here is that even though we're using information, As an example, it's important for us as competent security professionals to understand that we are talking not only about protecting information. Information is only one example. of valuable assets that we need to protect using, for example, these services in any organization. Now, the reason why we're using information as an example of a valuable asset is because that is specifically mentioned in the exam outline. But as competent security professionals, one of the things that we need to always focus on and highlight is that security needs to protect all valuable assets of the organization from these perspectives, including information, but anything that represents value to an organization. is considered to be a asset. In fact, the word asset is defined as anything that represents value to an organization. And as we've said, and we will say many times, an asset is always protected based on its value. That's what dictates, for example, the level of security that we need to apply to valuable assets. We need to understand the value of that asset so that we can protect that value. Now, obviously, security is not in the best position to understand the value of an asset. The owners are. And that's why this concept of ownership in security is very important. In fact, of paramount importance to the success of security. And that message needs to come from security. Hopefully, the executive and the CEO, for example, that this concept of ownership is really the basis for how we protect all assets of the organization through security and these security services that you see here. So these can be referred to as the five pillars. of security used to protect and address the value of really valuable assets within the organization itself. Now let's look at each of these in more detail. Although again, I think most of us are pretty familiar with the focus of each one of these. But for example, confidentiality. That really means that if we are protecting an asset from a confidentiality perspective, we're trying to make sure that it's protected from the perspective of only allowing access to those that have been authorized to be able to access that asset. And again, we are using information as an example because that's what the exam may use. But we need to focus, for example, on all valuable assets. I want to emphasize that important perspective that we need to have even though we're using information here as a really good example of a valuable asset that obviously would need to be protected from a confidentiality perspective. So confidentiality controls, are trying to prevent the unauthorized access to those assets so that only authorized individuals and subjects that have a need to know can access that asset based on, again, need to know and least privilege concepts that we're going to talk about later on. Now, unfortunately, from a security perspective, we haven't really done a good job of explaining what security really means. and most people when you mention the word security they often think of confidentiality controls but it's important to highlight that today especially today in our environments it may be very important and in fact just as important to protect assets including information from these other pillars or perspectives that you see here. The next one, of course, is what we call integrity. And again, this is one that we haven't really done a good job of explaining to our stakeholders. But when we apply integrity controls, The best way to explain this and to understand it is to reflect on words like accuracy and meaningfulness and timeliness and currency, for example. In fact, again, if we're using information as an example, when we apply integrity controls to that information, We're trying to make that information more accurate, more timely, more current, more meaningful, because if we can make information or any other asset, more accurate, more timely, more current, more meaningful, it will drive better business decisions. So that's the whole focus of integrity controls. We're trying to make assets more accurate, for example. The next one, which I think all of us are also very familiar with, is availability. And this is probably the one that is easiest to understand from a stakeholder perspective. We're simply trying to make sure that valuable assets are always available to us whenever we need them. or whenever they're needed by our stakeholders that are authorized to access, for example, those assets. And so typically the way that we best address availability needs is through redundancy. Cost-effective redundancy controls is what we typically apply to valuable assets to make sure that they are always available whenever they're needed. Now here are the two that the industry has really added and it's important again for us to reflect on these as being just as important as your traditional CIA triad that you see here. But the next one is what we call authenticity. Now, pay attention here because this does not say authentication. So don't confuse it with authentication that is usually used in the context of access control, like how we authenticate individuals or subjects to be able to access something. That is not what this is focused on. In fact, if you look at the definition here, We've highlighted something called proof of origin. Authenticity really means that we can prove the source of something. We know where it came from. we can authenticate the source of where something originated or came from. And that may be a very important security service to address in today's environments. And here's a practical example of where this might come in. Let's say that in our organization, we want to patch our Microsoft operating systems. We want to make sure that that patch is actually coming from Microsoft and not some other organization or website pretending to be Microsoft. And so we may need to prove the authenticity of that patch. In other words, we may need to prove the origin of that patch, that it actually originated and came from Microsoft and not something else pretending to be Microsoft. Now, this service, authenticity, also referred to as proof of origin in the industry, is best addressed in the cryptography section that we're going to cover, obviously, when we cover domain number three. Because the best way to prove authenticity or proof of origin is to use cryptography services. So we will describe this in detail when we cover cryptography in this course. pillar of security or important focus of security is what we refer to as or what the industry refers to as non-repudiation. The key word here is the word repudiation. And if you look that up in a dictionary, it actually means to be able to deny. That's what to repudiate means. Repudiation means to be able to deny. So the important security focus here is non-repudiation, which means the inability to deny. So if a subject or someone initiates or starts something, they cannot say later that it wasn't them that actually did that. That's the whole focus of non-repudiation. We want to be able to prevent subjects or applications or services from being able to deny that they actually did something or initiated something or started something. Now this, just like authenticity, is best addressed when we talk about cryptography because the best way to address non-repudiation is to use digital signatures. and digital signatures are a direct service that is provided through cryptography. So when we get to the cryptography section, we're going to explain both authenticity and non-repudiation in detail. Now what you need to remember about non-repudiation is that there are two flavors of it. There is non-repudiation of origin and non-repudiation of delivery. And it's important for us to remember the correct terminology. And again, Anything that is addressed on the CISSP exam comes from the actual security industry, if you will. It's not ISC2 that comes up with terminology that you need to understand as far as the exam is concerned. It all comes from the industry and that creates something called the common body of knowledge of security, which in turn is what's addressed through the exam and the exam outline, for example, and the exam questions. All right, so the correct terminology as it relates to non-repudiation. is that there are two flavors, non-repudiation of origin, which basically means that the sender or the initiator of something cannot say that it wasn't them that sent something or initiated something later. will be able to prove it that it was them through digital signatures. That's non-repudiation of origin. Non-repudiation of delivery is the other flavor. And that basically means that, for example, a receiver of a message or someone that has received something cannot say later that it wasn't them that actually received that particular message or data. We can prove it, again, through data. digital signatures. And again, we'll talk about this in detail when we get to cryptography. All right. So these are referred to as the five pillars of not just information security. We're using information as an example of a valuable asset. These are important focuses that we as competent security professionals need to be able to address based on the requirements and the value of all assets that represent value to the organization itself. Now, one of the things that I know is important to reflect on is that we use words like security and protect and so on. And in fact, that's what the organization wants us to do, to be able to protect valuable assets by really applying any one or more or all of these pillars or services that you see here. But if you think about this from a stakeholder or management or CEO perspective, What those stakeholders really want security to be able to do is to add value, not just protect the value of assets to the level that it exists at. Security should be able to actually add value, not just protect it. And so that's a really important focus. In other words, if you're explaining these services to a stakeholder management, a CEO, for example, they'll want to know how by applying these types of security controls, we can actually add value to assets. And so I'm going to give you an example here that may be important to remember. And I'm going to use integrity as an example. How do we actually add value to an asset by applying integrity controls, right? We're not just protecting it. We're actually increasing the value of that asset by applying integrity controls to it. And if you remember just a few minutes ago, how I explained what integrity controls really mean, it means we're trying to address the accuracy, the timeliness, the currency, the meaningfulness of a particular asset. And yes, let's use information as an example. Let's say we have a database that contains obviously very important information that is being used to drive business decisions. If you explain to a CEO, for example, that because of our integrity controls that we apply to that database, it makes information more accurate, more timely, more current, more meaningful. That's what our integrity controls are doing. They're making sure that the information that's contained within that database is always more accurate, more timely. It reflects current trends and analyses and information that we reflect on. In other words, by applying those security controls that are focused on integrity, we're making that information more accurate. more timely, more current. Because if that information is used to drive decisions, because it is more accurate, more timely, more current, it will drive better business decisions. Again, the best way to think of this is if you think about accuracy. If you can make an asset more accurate, it becomes more valuable to an organization. We've increased its value to the point where, for example, it's driving better business decisions. It's making the organization itself more accurate, more timely, more valuable. So by applying integrity controls, because we are making things more accurate, that adds and increases the value of that asset. So important to reflect on this. as far as what security does today. We don't just minimize risk. We don't just protect information. We protect all assets. But when we use the word protect, It actually doesn't just mean protecting assets to the level that is required based on their value, but we're actually trying to increase the value by applying these controls. And we've used integrity as an example because that's an important one to reflect on.
